coredata.dc:title: Highly efficient photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B by conical graphene quantum dots/cerium oxide composite
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Hebing
Zhang, Hongjuan
Mo, Zunli
Guo, Ruibin
Liu, Nijuan
Jia, Qianqian
Gao, Qinqin
coredata.dc:description: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are graphene nanoparticles with a particle size of less than 100 nm. GQDs are used as sensitizers for semiconductor catalytic materials and have a significant enhancement effect on the catalytic performance of the catalyst. We have prepared GQDs/CeO2 composites to improve the catalytic performance of CeO2. GQDs in the GQDs/CeO2 composites were uniformly coated around each CeO2 crystal, which effectively increased the reactive sites and effective contact interfaces. The composite GQDs-4.5/CeO2 absorption edge was broadened to 475 nm, with extremely low electron-hole recombination rate and optimal photoelectric performance. The composite material formed a heterogeneous structure with staggered energy levels. The photogenerated electrons were transferred from the GQDs conduction band to the CeO2 conduction band, which effectively prolonged the carrier lifetime, showed enhanced photocatalytic activity, and decomposed Rhodamine B (RhB) photocatalytic reaction under visible light. The reaction rate constant was up to 13.8 times that of pure CeO2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Cerium oxide
Photocatalytic
Composite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic performance test of composite materials
Phase composition and morphology analysis of GQDs/CeO2 composites

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2019 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

item-info.ce:copyright: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD patterns of bare CeO2, GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-4.5/CeO2 and GQDs-6.0/CeO2. (B) The Williamson–Hall (β cos θ versus sin θ) plot of bare CeO2.
(A) FT-IR spectra of GQDs、bare CeO2 and GQDs-4.5/CeO2. (B) FT-IR spectra of composite GQDs/CeO2 with different GQDs content.
SEM images of (A, B) bare CeO2 and (C, D) GQDs-4.5/CeO2 composite at different scanning magnifications.
(A) SEM image of GQDs-4.5/CeO2 composite. (B) and (C) TEM images of GQDs-4.5/CeO2 composite. (D) TEM image of bare CeO2.
(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of bare CeO2, GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-4.5/CeO2 and GQDs-6.0/CeO2. (B) Tauc plot of bare CeO2, GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-4.5/CeO2 and GQDs-6.0/CeO2.
(A) Photoluminescence spectra of GQDs/CeO2 composites with different GQDs content and (B) partial enlargement.
J–V curves of GQDs/CeO2 composite materials and inset shows the momentary photoexcitation current spectrum of bare CeO2, GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-4.5/CeO2 and GQDs-6.0/CeO2.
Nuquist plots for the GQDs/CeO2 composite materials and bare CeO2.

(A) Photoinduced photodegradation of RhB with bare CeO2 and GQDs/CeO2 composite materials. (B) The linear fittings of the pseudo-firstorder kinetics model in degrading RhB under visible light irradiation.
Recycling performance of RhB in GQDs-4.5/CeO2 composite material removal solution.
The simplified mechanism for the photocatalytic activity of GQDs-4.5/CeO2 composite.
Comparison of the RhB photocatalytic degradation results of this study with the other literature investigations.

head.ce:title: Highly efficient photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B by conical graphene quantum dots/cerium oxide composite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Gansu Military and Civilian Integration Advanced Structural Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Lanzhou, 730070, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) are graphene nanoparticles with a particle size of less than 100 nm. GQDs are used as sensitizers for semiconductor catalytic materials and have a significant enhancement effect on the catalytic performance of the catalyst. We have prepared GQDs/CeO2 composites to improve the catalytic performance of CeO2. GQDs in the GQDs/CeO2 composites were uniformly coated around each CeO2 crystal, which effectively increased the reactive sites and effective contact interfaces. The composite GQDs-4.5/CeO2 absorption edge was broadened to 475 nm, with extremely low electron-hole recombination rate and optimal photoelectric performance. The composite material formed a heterogeneous structure with staggered energy levels. The photogenerated electrons were transferred from the GQDs conduction band to the CeO2 conduction band, which effectively prolonged the carrier lifetime, showed enhanced photocatalytic activity, and decomposed Rhodamine B (RhB) photocatalytic reaction under visible light. The reaction rate constant was up to 13.8 times that of pure CeO2.

ce:section.ce:para: In 2004, Geim and Novoselov carried out a well-known experiment at Manchester University to prepare monolayer graphite by stripping tape [1]. This was the first time that graphene had been synthesized by experimental method. Four years later, Ponomarenko and Scheedin successfully prepared GQDs on the basis of previous work on graphene, which broadened the research field of graphene materials [2]. GQDs are quasi-zero-dimensional nanomaterials. They have excellent quantum confinement and edge effects without the accumulation and aggregation between graphene layers due to strong intermolecular forces. The optoelectronic properties of GQDs are affected by the factors such as raw materials, synthesis route, molecular size and surface structure.

Graphene and their derivatives are outstanding in photocatalysis and photoelectrocatalysis [3]. Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and metal oxide based binary (rGO–TiO2/rGO–Fe3O4) and ternary (rGO–Fe3O4–TiO2) nanocomposites with enhanced photocatalytic and adsorption properties were successfully synthesized by a simple one-step solvothermal process [4]. GQDs are also used as sensitizer for semiconductor catalytic materials and have a significant enhancement effect on the catalytic performance of catalysts [5–9]. GQDs/Fe2O3 composites synthesized by one-step hydrothermal synthesis can enhance visible light photocatalytic activity on benzene and methanol degrading toxic gases [5]. GQDs/ZnO composites prepared by one-step hydrothermal method can also enhance photocatalytic activity under visible light [6]. Yu reported a low temperature preparation of GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites, and found that the unique upconversion photoluminescence behavior of GQDs and the high active sites of TiO2 nanosheets synergistically can enhance the photocatalytic ability of Methylene Blue (MB) [7]. The catalytic activity of N-GQDs is comparable to that of carbon-supported Pt, and it also shows improved Oxygen Reductive Reaction (ORR) catalytic activity [8]. The researchers used 1,3,6-trinitropropene as a raw material to prepare GQDs coupled with TiO2 in a one-step hydrothermal process. The composite obtained by this method has close interfacial contact and is effective for TiO2. Light absorption was extended into the visible region and enhanced [9].

China is rich in rare earth resources, of which cerium is the most abundant and cheapest. CeO2 is a widely used semiconductor material, which has been applied in the fields of environment, biology, catalysis and electronics [10–13]. Because of its unique 4f1, 5d1, 6s2 electronic arrangement structure, cerium element can exist in the form of +4 valence or +3 valence stably. Different valence states will affect the coordination structure of atoms. The unique valence configuration of cerium element is helpful to improve the properties of materials. CeO2 is a commonly used photocatalyst. Its crystal belongs to fluorite-type face-centered cubic structure (8 O2− ions around each Ce4+ ion). The light absorption threshold of CeO2 can reach 420 nm, which is higher than that of the commonly used semiconductor material titanium dioxide.

The morphology, size, specific surface area and oxygen vacancy of CeO2 affect its photocatalytic performance. Oxygen defect is related to the electronic structure of cerium ion. In the process of transforming of cerium ion with different valence states, oxygen vacancies can be directly involved in the reaction with the storage and release of oxygen. Therefore, oxygen vacancies show redox performance and have potential applications in catalysis and energy conversion [14]. Composite CeO2 semiconductor was prepared by various modifications. Pei [15] and others synthesized CeO2 nanorods by hydrothermal method for photocatalysis, which had good degradation effect on MB under visible light. In recent studies such as reported by Manish [16], an Ag/Au co-doped CeO2 was used to study the role of oxygen vacancies by photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants, and the degradation mechanism was proposed. Murugan [17] and others used pure alkali metal ions (Mg, Ca, Sr, Ba) doped cerium oxide nanostructures for methylene blue photocatalytic degradation, and found that the photocatalytic activity was enhanced compared with pure CeO2. Through conductive polymer supported CeO2 to enhance the treatment of pollutants in water, the polymer provides an effective electronic transport channel for CeO2 semiconductor to improve its catalytic activity [18]. Improvement in the catalytic activity of nanostructured cerium oxides (CeOx) was attained by incorporating in-situ generated heteroatom-(N and S)-doped carbon quantum dots/reduced graphene oxide (HDCQD@RGO) nanohybrid catalyst for the degradation of organic pollutants [19].

Although CeO2 has its own advantages and potential in catalytic applications, its wide band gap leads to unsatisfactory catalytic performance in practical applications. In this paper, GQDs/CeO2 composites were successfully prepared by the combination of CeO2 and GQDs. GQDs were successfully coated on the surface of CeO2 through hydrothermal process, which induced the assembly of CeO2 to form a more orderly conical structure, which could provide more effective contact interfaces and active sites for photocatalytic reaction. Compared with single CeO2, the GQDs/CeO2 composites have higher photocatalytic degradation rate of organic pollutants, and the addition of GQDs also makes the composites have excellent electrochemical properties.

Expanded graphite (HD 250 B5) were acquired from Qingdao Nanye Hongda Graphite Products Co., Ltd. N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) (73 wt%) were purchased from Lianlong Bohua Medical Chemical Co., Ltd. Concentrated H2SO4 (98 wt%) was obtained from Chengdu Linjiang Chemical Plant. H3PO4 (98 wt%), HNO3 (68 wt%) and NH3·H2O (aqueous solution, 25 wt%) were purchased from Yantai Shuangshuang Chemical Co., Ltd. Potassium permanganate were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. H2O2 (aqueous solution, 30 wt%) was obtained from Baiyin Chemical Reagent Factory. Hydrazine hydrate (N2H4·H2O) (aqueous solution, 85 wt%) was obtained from Tianjin Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. CeO2 were acquired from Gansu Rare Earth New Materials Co., Ltd.

GQDs were prepared from three-dimensional network graphene (3DNG) by “top-down” method. The specific implementation process was as follows: Graphene was prepared by expanded graphite from liquid phase stripping, then using graphene as raw material, graphite oxide (GO) was prepared by modified Hummers' method [20]. GO and ultrapure water were mixed in a ratio of 1 mg/mL. After ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min, ammonia water (5% of volume fraction) and reducing agent hydrazine hydrate (2.5% of volume fraction) were added. The solution was transferred into a PTFE-lined high-pressure reactor for 12 h at 180 °C, and 3DNG were obtained. 0.01 g 3DNG and 20 mL concentrated nitric acid were mixed in a round-bottom flask, then refluxed at 150 °C for 20 h to obtain light yellow graphene quantum dot dispersions. After removing the condensation device, and until the liquid evaporated completely, light brown graphene quantum dots were synthesized. The solution of graphene quantum dots showed green fluorescence under the irradiation of ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm, and the yield was about 75%.

GQDs/CeO2 composites were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal method. The raw material CeO2 was directly purchased without further treatment. 4.0 g of CeO2 was dissolved into 20 mL of deionized water. And then different amounts of GQDs were added to above solution. After 30 min of ultrasonic dispersion, it was stirred for 2 h. The mixture was transferred into the reactor and heated to 100 °C for 3 h. The product was centrifugally separated at 10000 rpm, washing with ultra-pure water and freeze-drying for 12 h. According to the different amount of GQDs added in the reaction system, the samples were labeled as GQDs-1.5/CeO2, GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-4.5/CeO2, GQDs-6.0/CeO2, GQDs-7.5/CeO2, GQDs-9.0/CeO2, representing 1.5 wt%, 3.0 wt%, 4.5 wt%, 6.0 wt%, 7.5 wt%, 9.0 wt% respectively.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of material was carried out by D8A A25 X-ray diffraction (Brooke (Beijing) Technology Co., Ltd.). The test results were compared with XRD standard PDF cards. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was characterized by EQUINOX55 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer. X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was used to precisely analyze the qualitative, quantitative, chemical valence and valence states of elements that characterize the surface or interface of materials. In the experiment, the element analysis of the samples was carried out by PHI5702 multifunctional electronic energy spectrometer of American Physical Electronics Company. Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) from ZEISS Company of Germany was used for characterization. The Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) used in the test was Hitachi-7650 produced by HITACHI Company of Japan, which was obtained under 120 kV acceleration voltage. In order to analyze the absorption range of photocatalyst to light and calculate the energy bandwidth of catalyst by diffuse reflectance spectra, the ultraviolet–visible region was measured on Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrophotometer equipped with integrating sphere. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) was carried out at 200–800 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) of the material was tested on a FluoroMax-4 fluorescence spectrometer made by HORIBA.

The electrochemical measurement system was based on Shanghai Chenhua CHI660E electrochemical workstation and 300W xenon lamp. The Pt wire electrode was a three-electrode battery with the opposite electrode and Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode. 0.5 mol/L of Na2SO4 was used as electrolyte. The working electrode was prepared by dispersing 10 mg composite material in 495 μL isopropanol, 495 μL ultrapure water and 10 μL Nafion mixed solution. After half an hour of ultrasound, 100 μL sample was uniformly dripped onto 1.5 × 1.5 cm2 conductive glass (ITO) conductive surface. After drying at room temperature for 24 h, the samples were assembled and measured in a three-electrode system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic performance test of composite materials
ce:section.ce:para: Shanghai Lanyi LY-GHX-Xe-300 photocatalytic reactor was used. The xenon lamp source was borrowed from HSX-F300 xenon lamp of Beijing Newbit Technology Co., Ltd. The source used filter to filter out ultraviolet light (λ < 400 nm). The reactor circulated water kept constant temperature and stirred continuously. The xenon lamp was irradiated under dark conditions and visible light, respectively. The operation steps are as follows: RhB solution with 10 mg/L concentration was used as reaction solution, 50 mL RhB solution was put in quartz well of photochemical reaction, 25 mg photocatalytic material was taken, and magnetic stirring for 1 h under dark conditions to establish adsorption equilibrium of dye molecules on catalyst surface and solution. Then, the Xenon lamp visible light was turned on and the samples of about 4 mL were absorbed in the centrifugal tube every 30 min during the photocatalytic experiment. The supernatant was obtained by centrifugation at 3000 rpm. Next the absorbance of the centrifugal solution at the wavelength of λ max = 554 nm (RhB maximum absorption wavelength) was measured in the UV-2550 visible spectrophotometer, and the liquid was obtained. The absorbance to be measured (Ax = 0, 1, …) was determined according to the concentration and initial reaction of RhB in the solution after the reaction.

The degradation rate of RhB by photocatalyst was calculated and used to evaluate the degradation of dye by photocatalyst under visible light. The calculation formula is as follows:
η % =
(
C
0
− C
C
0
)
× 100 %
where η% is the degradation rate of RhB, C0 is the initial concentration of RhB, and C is the concentration of RhB at a certain time interval of photoreaction.

The conversion of absorbance (A) and dye concentration (C) is based on the formula C/C0 = A/A0. A is the instant absorbance of dyes at different time intervals, and A0 is the initial absorbance after dark adsorption. In addition, the kinetic fitting of degradation process is also used to evaluate the performance of photocatalytic materials. The kinetic formula is as follows:
ln (C/C0) = k × t
where t is the time interval of reaction and k is the rate of photocatalytic reaction.

After each experiment, the remaining photocatalytic materials were recovered, washed by centrifugal water and dried. The reusability data of the catalyst were obtained by repeating the above steps as raw materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase composition and morphology analysis of GQDs/CeO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structure and phase purity of the composites were analyzed by X-ray diffraction. The XRD spectra of pure CeO2, GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-4.5/CeO2 and GQDs-6.0/CeO2 are shown in Fig. 1 . The crystals of CeO2 have diffraction peaks at 28.5°, 33.1°, 47.51°, 56.4°, 59.1°, 69.45°, 76.7°and 79.1°, which can be indexed to the standard spectra of cubic fluorite phase (JCPDS No. 34–0394). They are assigned to (111), (200), (220), (311), (222), (400), (331) and (420) crystal planes, respectively. The results show that CeO2 is highly crystalline and matches the results observed by TEM. The crystal diffraction patterns of GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-4.5/CeO2 and GQDs-6.0/CeO2 are consistent with those of pure CeO2. No diffraction peaks of GQDs are found, which may be related to the low amount of GQDs, and the amount of GQDs in the composites has no effect on the diffraction patterns of the components of the products. In addition, the peak intensity of (111) crystal plane diffraction of pure CeO2 is the highest, when combined with GQDs, the intensity of diffraction peak decreases. The average crystallite size D and strain η were determined by making a Williamson–Hall plot (Fig. 1B), the relation being β cos θ = (0.89λ/D) + η sin θ (λ = 1.54056Å for Cu Kα), θ is Bragg diffraction angle and β is the broadening of the diffraction peak measured at FWHM (in radians), using the values for the diffraction peak width (β) obtained after correction for instrumental broadening. Based on XRD data, the crystallite size of the powder obtained is 11.58 nm. The value of the strain ‘η’ is 0.09375, which is small, indicating negligible strain induced broadening of the diffraction patterns [21,22].

FTIR characterization of raw materials and GQDs/CeO2 composites with different contents of GQDs was carried out to analyze the infrared absorption characteristics of GQDs/CeO2 composites. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the infrared spectra of GQDs-4.5/CeO2 shows that the skeleton vibration peak of Ce–O appears in the fingerprint region, and the characteristic absorption peak of GQDs also appears, indicating that the combination of GQDs and CeO2 is successful. In Fig. 2B, the intensity of characteristic peaks of GQDs in the infrared spectra from GQDs-1.5/CeO2 to GQDs-9.0/CeO2 increases with the increase of GQDs content in composites with gradient of GQDs content change. The peaks at 1383 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of C–N/N–H bond doped with nitrogen element, 1615 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of CC bond [23], 1730 cm−1 belongs to the absorption peak of CO bond on carbonyl group, 2816 cm−1 belongs to the peak of sp3 hybrid C–H bond, 1103 cm−1 belongs to the absorption peak of CO bond on carbonyl group. Vibrational absorption peaks of C–O–C functional groups and stretching vibration of O–H bonds of water molecules absorbed by samples at 3400 cm−1. The above analysis shows that GQDs/CeO2 composites with different GQDs dosage have been successfully prepared.

To investigate the chemical nature of the GQDs/CeO2 composites, the XPS measurements of Ce, C, O and N binding energies were carried out (Fig. 3 ). The full spectrum analysis of GQDs/CeO2 composites (Fig. 3A) shows that the materials are mainly composed of Ce, C, O and N, which is consistent with the input results of the original materials. The results show that the atomic contents of Ce, C, O and N are 23.42%, 28.55%, 46.30% and 1.73%, respectively, indicating that GQDs contain more elements, which are related to the bonding mode of composites and the testing conditions of XPS. For example, GQDs are more wrapped on the surface of cerium dioxide, and XPS test data are more from samples surface. Using XPSpeak 4.1 software to deconvolute the characteristic peaks of elements, we know that oxygen may be derived from GQDs and CeO2, while carbon and nitrogen in composites come from GQDs, and cerium should come from cerium dioxide. Fig. 3B shows the XPS spectra of C1s with high resolution in GQDs/CeO2 composites. The absorption peaks at 284.7 eV and 285.2 eV correspond to CC and C–C bond absorption in graphene quantum dot structures, respectively. The binding energy at 285.9 eV and 289.0 eV belongs to C–N/C–O and CO in GQDs. Fig. 3C shows O1s high resolution XPS. The absorption peak of binding energy at 532.5 eV belongs to CO bond on GQDs. The binding energy at 529.7 eV and 531.5 eV corresponds to lattice oxygen of Ce4+-O2- and Ce3+-O2- in CeO2 respectively [16]. In the high resolution spectrum of N1s in Fig. 3D, the binding energies are 399.4 eV, 400.5 eV and 401.4 eV, respectively, corresponding to pyridine N, pyrrolic N and graphite N [24]. Fig. 3E is a high resolution XPS of Ce3d in composites. According to the literature reports, we find that the spectrum of Ce3d can be fitted as a peak, marked as “V” and “U”, respectively. Generally, the peak “V” represents the characteristic peak of 3d5/2, and the peak “U” represents the characteristic peak of 3d3/2. Under “V” and “U”, there are different subscripts, in which V0, V′, U0 and U′ are the characteristic peaks of Ce3+ and V, V′, V′, U′, U′, and U′, respectively, are the characteristic peaks of Ce4+ [25,26]. The first nine fitting peaks of Ce3d are found in Fig. 3E, which have been marked in the graph. The binding energies from small to large are 880.1, 882.9, 886.2, 889.4, 898.6, 899.4, 901.2, 906.5 and 907.8 eV. It can be seen from the results that CeO2 mainly exists in the form of Ce4+ and contains different contents of Ce3+. The coexistence and transformation of different valence states of CeO2 elements are easy to form oxygen vacancies. The direct participation of oxygen vacancies in the reaction will be of great benefit to the improvement of photocatalytic performance in subsequent experiments.

In this study, we used SEM to characterize the intuitive morphology of raw materials and composites. In Fig. 4 , we found that the morphology of the composites changed greatly compared with the raw materials. Specifically, Fig. 4A and B are photos of raw materials CeO2 at different magnification. At low magnification, CeO2 nanoparticles aggregate more irregularly and contain some short rod-like structures. At high magnification, we find that these CeO2 nanoparticles are complete cubic fluorite crystals, as shown in Fig. 4D. The results are consistent. These CeO2 nanoparticles are aggregated because of their large surface energy. When GQDs were added into the material to form composite GQDs-4.5/CeO2, as shown in Fig. 4C, GQDs could not be observed in the figure because of their several nanometer scales. Compared with CeO2, the macro-morphology of composites has changed from disorderly accumulation to relatively orderly conical shape. This may be due to the fact that GQDs are coated on the surface of CeO2 after GQDs are added, which makes the surface energy change, while self-assembly becomes the morphological structure in Fig. 4D. In fact, it can change characteristics of CeO2, but its specific assembly mechanism is not very clear. From the catalytic performance analysis, the addition of GQDs reduces the energy band gap of CeO2, and the orderly morphology of CeO2 is conducive to the stable transmission of electrons, reduces the recombination probability of electron-hole pairs, and enhances the photocatalytic performance of the composites.

We used TEM to characterize the morphology and microstructure of CeO2 and GQDs-4.5/CeO2 composites. As illustrated in Fig. 5 B, the composite GQDs-4.5/CeO2 exhibits a complete conical morphology composed of CeO2 nanoparticles, which is consistent with the results observed in Fig. 5A. Fig. 5C shows the high-resolution transmission electron micrograph of the composite. From the figure, GQDs partially attached to CeO2 can be observed. The complete lattice fringes in the substrate are attributed to the raw material CeO2, which is consistent with the results of XRD. The lattice fringe regions of the GQDs indicated in the figure, whose scale and lattice spacing match the results of GQDs, indicate that GQDs are successfully compounded on the surface of CeO2 to form GQDs/CeO2 composites. The formation of this interesting morphological structure by CeO2 induced by GQDs can improve the photo-response performance of CeO2 and also facilitate the transmission of photogenerated electrons. The specific embodiment will be explained in the subsequent performance test. Fig. 5D shows the TEM image of the raw material CeO2. From the figure, we can observe that the CeO2 nanoparticles of the cubic fluorite phase are distributed on the carrier. Comparing the composite materials in Fig. 5B, we can visually find the two shapes. The difference in characteristics further indicates that the composite material is successfully prepared and a more optimized morphology structure is formed.

The performance of photoelectric conversion materials are mainly reflected in their ability to respond to light. The response to visible light is a major aspect of solar energy utilization, and it is also the direction of photoelectric conversion materials in the application field. Fig. 6 A shows the diffuse reflectance spectra of the composite GQDs/CeO2 we prepared. From the figure we observed that the absorption edge of the composite has a significant red shift compared to pure CeO2, where the absorption edge of CeO2 at 415 nm, the absorption edge of GQDs-4.5/CeO2 is 475 nm, which is mainly due to the action of the π-π conjugated structure in the GQDs. The broadening of the absorption edge of the composite material to ultraviolet light and visible light can effectively enhance the utilization of visible light compared with the raw material, thereby improving the catalytic efficiency of the composite material under visible light. Fig. 6B displays the Tauc plot arrangement of (ahv) 1/2 vs. photon energy for the bare CeO2, GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-4.5/CeO2 and GQDs-6.0/CeO2 catalysts. Fitting the linear region of the curve yields, a linear equation was obtained whose intercept on the x-axis is the value of forbidden band [27,28]. The forbidden bandwidth of the composite was reduced compared to the forbidden bandwidth of 3.1 eV of the raw material CeO2. The forbidden bandwidth values of GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-4.5/CeO2 and GQDs-6.0/CeO2 are 2.98, 2.42 and 2.63 eV, respectively (Fig. 6B). This result can be judged that GQDs have a great influence on the band gap value of CeO2, so GQDs-4.5/CeO2 composite has better visible light response than pure CeO2.

Through the above-mentioned SEM and TEM characterizations, we know that GQDs were wrapped on the surface of CeO2, and there was a close interface between them. In addition, from the characterization of UV–Vis absorption spectrum, we found that the band gap of the composites had changed more than that of pure ones. These characteristics reflect the photogenerated electrons on GQDs from the side. It can move freely to CeO2, and this transfer process will prolong the lifetime of electrons and holes. More probabilities of electrons and holes can more effectively reflect their properties and enhance the photoelectric properties of composites. According to previous studies, photoluminescence spectroscopy is usually used to demonstrate the relationship between the separation efficiency of electrons and holes [29]. Fig. 7 shows the photoluminescence spectra of pure CeO2 and GQDs/CeO2 composites at excitation wavelength of 320 nm. Pure CeO2 and composite materials have similar spectral characteristics, but the photoluminescence intensity of pure CeO2 is much higher than that of several composite materials, which indicates that the introduction of GQDs improves the separation efficiency of electrons and holes (Fig. 7A). In addition, it can be seen from Fig. 7B that the peak intensity of GQDs-4.5/CeO2 is the lowest in the photoluminescence spectra of composites with different GQDs content, which also shows that the composite GQDs-4.5/CeO2 has the best photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiency.

As shown in Fig. 8 , in photoelectric current density test shows that the voltage range is −0.7–0.9 V, and the load of material on the working electrode was 1 mg (100 μL, 10 mg/mL). The figure shows that the photocurrent value of all composites is larger than that of pure CeO2. When the content of GQDs increases to 4.5 wt%, the photocurrent value increases. When the composite content increases further from 4.5 wt% to 9.0 wt%, due to the slow process of electron injection, the effective connection of surface excited electrons is weakened and the electron-hole pairs are weakened. The possibility of recombination is higher, and the photocurrent density decreases. The photoelectrochemical properties of semiconductors mainly depend on the photoinduced electrons, the separation of photoelectron-hole pairs and the carrier migration rate. Fig. 8 shows the relationship between photocurrent density and time under intermittent visible light irradiation. The optical current densities of pure CeO2, GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-4.5/CeO2 and GQDs-6.0/CeO2 are 156, 524, 604 and 462 μA/cm2, respectively. The optimum value of GQDs composite is 4.5 wt%, and its photocurrent density is 3.9 times than that of pure CeO2. It is proved that GQDs/CeO2 has more photogenerated carrier generation under visible light irradiation when combined with graphene quantum dots. Light-induced electrons and holes generated on GQDs are transferred to the surface of CeO2, and the electrons and holes of GQDs are also complexed. Due to certain restrictions, the transmission path of photogenerated carriers is increased, and the photoelectric reactivity of GQDs/CeO2 is improved [17].

To further demonstrate the effect of the addition of GQDs on the reception of visible light and the separation and transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs in composites, we performed Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) measurement. Fig. 9 shows that CeO2 has the largest impedance radius under visible light irradiation with a bias of 0.5 V, indicating the highest charge transfer resistance. The impedance radii of CeO2, GQDs-1.5/CeO2, GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-4.5/CeO2, GQDs-6.0/CeO2, GQDs-7.5/CeO2 and GQDs-9.0/CeO2 are 265.6 Ω,184.8 Ω,142.4 Ω,126.5 Ω,170.9 Ω,228.0 Ω and 255.8 Ω respectively. Lower impedance resistance is conducive to higher photocurrent generation, which is consistent with the test results in Figs. 5–8. These results indicate that the composite GQDs exhibit better photogenerated carrier separation efficiency, fast electron-hole interface transfer, and the preparation of GQDs-4.5/CeO2 composite with the best electrochemical activity.

We studied the photocatalytic activity of CeO2 and the GQDs/CeO2 composites with different GQDs content by photocatalytic removal of RhB in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. In order to compare their catalytic activities, we also performed photocatalytic degradation experiments on RhB. As shown in Fig. 10 A, after 60 min dark conditions, we found that CeO2 and RhB were adsorbed and the difference in adsorption amount was small in this process, which was due to the size of GQDs. Relationships have little effect on the adsorption process. Under visible light irradiation, the degradation ability from large to small is: GQDs-4.5/CeO2, GQDs-3.0/CeO2, GQDs-6.0/CeO2, GQDs-7.5/CeO2, GQDs-1.5/CeO2, GQDs-9.0/CeO2, CeO2. Among them, GQDs-4.5/CeO2 shows the strongest catalytic activity, which indicates that the optimum compound amount was 4.5 wt% when combined with GQDs, and CeO2 could best cooperate with GQDs to exhibit the highest catalytic activity.

Fig. 10B shows the kinetics of photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutant RhB by blank and catalyst CeO2 and GQDs/CeO2 composites under visible light. According to the kinetic formula, the k (apparent rate constant) value of GQDs-4.5/CeO2 is 1.24, which is 13.8 times than that of pure CeO2 (k = 0.09). The comprehensive kinetic analysis shows that the addition of GQDs greatly enhances the photocatalytic performance of the composites, which mainly due to the widening of the absorption wavelength range of the visible light region by GQDs, the separation rate of electron holes, and the prolongation of holes and electrons. At the same time, the synergistic effect of GQDs and CeO2 on the high activity sites enhances the photocatalytic performance of GQDs/CeO2, which is consistent with the above characterization results.

Photocatalytic stability of composite materials is an important performance characteristic in practical application [30]. Fig. 11 is the test data of visible light photocatalytic cycling performance of composite catalyst GQDs-4.5/CeO2. After each end, the product was centrifugally separated and washed and dried repeatedly. It can be seen from the figure that the RhB removal rate of GQDs-4.5/CeO2 is 96.9% after three consecutive photodegradation reactions for 300 min, which indicates that the composite catalyst has good stability. This result is much superior to the reported analogous photocatalyts, such as CeO2/Bi2O3 nanospheres [31], m-BiVO4@CeO2 hollow microspheres [32], 0.4CeO2/0.6Bi2WO6 composite film [33], CeO2–ZnO composite nanofibers [34], TiO2/CeO2/Bi2O3 composite [35] as listed in Table 1 .

Based on the above characterization and the reported literature [14,15,30], we proposed the possible mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of RhB by GQDs/CeO2 composite. Fig. 12 is a schematic diagram of the degradation mechanism of GQDs/CeO2 composites. Under visible light irradiation, the excitation produces electron-hole pairs. CeO2 also has a weak response to visible light. Photoelectrons are generated on GQDs and CeO2, respectively, and transfer from valence bands (VB) to their respective conduction bands (CB), while some photoelectrons are transferred from GQDs to CeO2. The photoelectrons transferred to the CeO2 conduction band are captured by dissolved oxygen in water to form superoxide anions (·O2−) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH). Contrary to the direction of photoelectron transfer, photogenerated holes transfer from the valence band (VB) of CeO2 to the valence band of GQDs. Some of the holes (h+) in the valence band of CeO2 and the holes transferred to the valence band of GQDs can oxidize RhB molecules directly, and can also react with water molecules (H2) and hydroxyl radicals (OH−) in solution to produce hydroxyl self-generation. The radical (·OH) participates in the oxidative degradation of RhB. The internal field at the GQDs/CeO2 interface promotes the formation of charge transfer state and the separation of photogenerated carriers in the composite structure, which improves the photoinduced excitation of the material.

GQDs is a carbon nano-material with excellent performance, CeO2 has outstanding photocatalytic potential. GQDs/CeO2 composites photocatalyst were successfully prepared by hydrothermal method using nano-ceria and GQDs as raw materials. Based on SEM and TEM characterization, it was found that the morphology of CeO2 became a conical morphology by self-assembly with the addition of GQDs. This orderly morphology had a positive effect on the performance of photoelectric properties. Further optical characterization showed that the addition of GQDs enlarged the UV–Vis absorption edge and visible light absorption of GQDs/CeO2. The band gap of composite GQDs/CeO2 was lower than that of CeO2 as a whole, and the separation rate of electrons and holes was also greatly improved. Among them, the absorption edge of GQDs-4.5/CeO2 was 475 nm, and the band gap was 2.42 eV. The electrochemical measurements showed that the composites had better photoelectric activity, and the maximum photocurrent density was 604 μA/cm2. In the actual visible light photocatalytic testing, we found that GQDs/CeO2 composite had more advantages in photocatalytic performance, and when the GQDs content was 4.5 wt%, the apparent rate constant was the highest. CeO2 was also responsive to visible light. The existence of GQDs made photogenerated electrons-holes had higher separation efficiency, and the utilization of visible light improved. According to the results of the current study, GQDs/CeO2 nanocomposite is an effective catalyst for the decontamination of rhodamine B and other similar low biodegradable pollution and also real industrial wastewaters.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51262027), the State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing in NWPU (SKLSP201754), the Science and Technology Project Gansu Province (17YF1GA017), the Research Project of Higher Education in Gansu Province (2017A-002), and the Science and Technology Project Gansu Province (17JR5RA082).
